title: Seeing Light During Kundalini Awakening: Phosphenes, Visions, or Something Else?

body:
Seeing Light During Kundalini Awakening: Phosphenes, Visions, or Something Else?

You're probably reading this because you've seen something. Something weird. A flash, a glow, a whole damn kaleidoscope behind your eyelids or even with your eyes open. And you're terrified, right? You're wondering if you're going crazy, if your brain's breaking, or if you've tapped into something truly wild. I get it. I’ve been there. I know what it's like to be utterly alone at 3 AM, staring at the ceiling, seeing lights dance and feeling like your entire reality is unraveling. It's a lonely place, a confusing place, and honestly, it can be a terrifying one. But here's the thing: you're not alone, and you're not necessarily losing your mind. What you're experiencing could very well be part of a kundalini awakening, and those lights? They're a common, albeit disorienting, symptom.

I'm Kalesh. I went through my own kundalini crisis, and it nearly broke me. I saw lights, heard sounds, felt energy coursing through my body like an electrical storm. I was convinced I was dying, or worse, that I was irrevocably insane. So, I'm not here to sugarcoat it or give you some fluffy spiritual platitudes. I'm here to tell you what I know, what I've learned, and what might be happening to you. Let's talk about these lights. Are they just phosphenes - those little sparks you see when you rub your eyes? Are they true visions? Or is it something else entirely? The truth is, it can be all of the above, and more.

The Spectrum of Light Experiences

When you're going through a kundalini awakening, your nervous system is basically getting a massive, involuntary upgrade. It's like trying to run super high-voltage electricity through old, frayed wires. Things are going to flicker, spark, and sometimes, explode. Your brain, your optic nerves, your entire sensory system - it's all being stimulated in ways it's never been before. So, when you see lights, it's not just your imagination. There's a physiological component, and there's a spiritual one. They're intertwined, and untangling them can be a real headache, especially when you're already feeling overwhelmed.

I remember one night, I woke up feeling this intense pressure behind my eyes, and then it was like someone switched on a disco ball inside my head. Flashes of color, geometric patterns, pulsing lights - it was intense. I thought I was having a stroke. My heart was pounding, I couldn't breathe, and I just wanted it to stop. But it didn't. It became a regular occurrence. Sometimes it was soft, a gentle glow. Other times, it was like fireworks. You know that feeling, don't you? That feeling of being totally out of control of your own senses? It's unsettling. It's terrifying. And it's normal - for this process, anyway.

  • Phosphenes on Steroids: You know how if you press on your eyeballs, you see little squiggles of light? Those are phosphenes. They're caused by mechanical stimulation of your retina. During kundalini, your internal energy is stimulating those same pathways, but with way more intensity. It's not just pressure; it's subtle electrical currents, changes in blood flow, shifts in nerve activity. So you might see sparks, swirls, dots, or even more complex patterns. They can appear behind closed eyelids, or sometimes, even with your eyes open, making the world seem to shimmer or glow.
  • Internal Light Shows: Then there's the more organized light. I'm talking about internal visions of light. These aren't just random flashes; they can be structured. You might see mandalas, tunnels of light, bright white or golden light that seems to emanate from within. These often come with a feeling of peace, expansion, or deep insight, even if the rest of your experience is pure hell. I've heard people describe seeing a "third eye" opening, or a vortex of light in their forehead. It's wild, and it feels incredibly real.
  • Auric Perceptions: Some people start seeing auras around people, animals, or objects. It's like a subtle glow or a shimmering field of energy. This isn't just your brain making things up; it's your visual system becoming more sensitive to frequencies of light and energy that it usually filters out. It's like your internal filters are breaking down, allowing more information to flood in. It can be beautiful, but it can also be overwhelming, especially if you're suddenly seeing things that no one else around you seems to notice.

It's a lot, I know. It's a lot to process. And it can make you question everything you thought you knew about reality. But remember, this isn't necessarily a sign of pathology. It's a sign that something deep is happening within you. Your system is recalibrating, and light is often one of the first things people notice. It's like the universe is trying to get your attention, saying, "Hey, something big is going on here."

The Biology Behind the Brilliance (and the Fear)

So, what exactly is going on in your brain and body when these lights appear? It's not just 'spiritual energy' - though that's a part of it. There's real, tangible physiology at play. When kundalini energy starts to move, it stimulates your nervous system in ways that aren't fully understood by Western science yet, but researchers like Lee Sannella have documented these phenomena for decades. If you want to dive deeper into the clinical descriptions, I'd highly recommend The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link). It's a lifesaver for understanding you're not alone in these wild experiences.

Your brain is an electrical organ, right? Every thought, every feeling, every sensation is an electrical impulse. Kundalini is basically a supercharge of that electrical system. It's like plugging a massive power generator into a regular house wiring. Things are going to short circuit, lights are going to flicker, and sometimes, you're going to see sparks. Those sparks? They're the lights you're seeing.

  • Pineal Gland Activation: The pineal gland, often called the "third eye," is a tiny gland in the center of your brain. It produces melatonin, which regulates sleep, but it's also been linked to spiritual experiences and visions. When kundalini energy moves through the crown chakra and down into the pineal area, it can stimulate this gland, leading to vivid internal light shows, geometric patterns, and a sense of expanded awareness. It's like your internal projector is being switched on, and it's showing you things you've never seen before.
  • Retinal Stimulation: Your retinas are packed with photoreceptor cells. Even without external light, these cells can be stimulated by internal electrical impulses or subtle changes in blood flow. During a kundalini awakening, the sheer amount of energy moving through your head and nervous system can cause this internal stimulation, leading to phosphenes or more complex light patterns. It's not external light; it's your own internal biology generating the experience.
  • Synesthesia-like Effects: Sometimes, the senses cross over. You might hear a sound and see a color, or feel an emotion and perceive a light. This is a bit like synesthesia, where one sensory input triggers another. During kundalini, the barriers between your senses can become porous, leading to these blended experiences. So, a surge of energy might not just feel like energy; it might also look like a flash of light.

Does that land for you? It's not just woo-woo stuff; there's a biological basis for a lot of what you're seeing. It doesn't make it any less overwhelming, but it can help to ground you a little, knowing that your body isn't just randomly breaking down. It's reacting to an intense internal process. It's trying to cope with the sheer volume of energy and information that's suddenly coming online.

Visions and Their Meanings: More Than Just Blips

So, beyond the random flashes and phosphenes, what about the more structured light experiences? The visions? These are where things get even more interesting, and often, more confusing. You might see symbols, archetypal figures made of light, entire scenes playing out in your mind's eye, or even geometric patterns that feel like they hold ancient wisdom. These aren't just random brain glitches; they often carry meaning, even if you can't figure it out at 3 AM when you're sweating through your sheets.

I've seen spirals of light that felt like the entire universe unfolding. I've seen figures made purely of light, communicating without words. It's deep, and it can leave you feeling incredibly small and incredibly connected at the same time. The question is, what do you do with it? How do you make sense of it all?

Decoding the Internal Light Show

Truth is, you don't always have to "decode" it right away. Sometimes, the experience itself is the message. But if you're seeing recurring symbols or patterns, it's worth paying attention. What do they feel like? What emotions do they evoke? Do they seem to be guiding you or showing you something about yourself or the world?

  • Symbols of Unity: Many people report seeing mandalas, yantras, or other sacred geometric patterns made of light. These often represent the interconnectedness of all things, the balance of opposites, or the universal order. They can be incredibly calming and reassuring, even in the midst of chaos.
  • Guiding Lights: Sometimes, the light appears as a path, a tunnel, or a guiding star. It can feel like it's leading you somewhere, even if you don't know where that is. This can be a spiritual guide, an aspect of your higher self, or simply the path your awakening is taking. Trust the feeling it evokes. If it feels benevolent, allow yourself to be open to it.
  • Insights and Revelations: The light might illuminate certain truths for you. You might suddenly understand a complex concept, remember something forgotten, or see your life's purpose with crystal clarity. These light-based insights can be incredibly powerful, shifting your perspective in an instant.

It's important to remember that not everything you see is a literal message. Sometimes, it's just your system processing energy. Other times, it's a symbolic representation of what's happening within you. Don't get too hung up on trying to interpret every single flash. Just observe, acknowledge, and let it be. The meaning often unfolds over time, not in an instant. This is a marathon, not a sprint, and you're learning a whole new language of perception.

Coping When the Lights Get Too Bright

Okay, so you're seeing lights. Maybe they're beautiful, maybe they're terrifying, maybe they're just confusing. What do you do when it feels like too much? Because believe me, it can feel like too much. I've been there, curled up in a ball, wishing for darkness, wishing for silence, wishing for normalcy. But normalcy, as you knew it, is probably gone for a while. And that's okay. You're adapting to a new normal. Here's what helped me, and what might help you.

  • Grounding, Grounding, Grounding: When your head is full of light and your energy is soaring, you need to ground yourself. This means connecting back to your body and the physical world. Walk barefoot on the earth. Eat nourishing, heavy foods - root vegetables, stews, anything that feels solid. Take a warm bath. Do gentle stretches or yoga. Spend time in nature. The more you can connect with the earth, the less unmoored you'll feel.
  • Darkness and Quiet: While the light can be deep, sometimes you just need a break. Create a sacred space that's dark, quiet, and calm. Turn off screens. Meditate in a dimly lit room. Wear an eye mask if the internal light is too much. Give your nervous system a chance to rest and integrate. Too much stimulation, even beautiful stimulation, can be overwhelming.
  • Don't Fight It, Don't Force It: This is a big one. Trying to stop the lights, or trying to make them happen, will only add to your distress. Surrender to the experience. Allow it to be there without judgment. "Okay, brain, you're doing your light show again. Noted." Just observe it without getting caught up in the fear or the excitement. Acceptance is key here.
  • Journal Your Experiences: Write down what you see, how it feels, what thoughts or emotions come up. This isn't just for interpretation; it's a way to externalize the experience, to get it out of your head and onto paper. It can help you track patterns, process emotions, and feel a little more in control, even if you're just documenting the chaos.
  • Seek Support (the Right Kind): This is vital. You need someone who understands, or at least believes you. Not everyone will. Your doctor might look at you like you have two heads. Your friends might think you're having a breakdown. Find communities online, spiritual counselors, or therapists who specialize in spiritual emergence. Someone who's been through it or has guided others through it. For more on finding support and understanding the process, I found When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) incredibly helpful. They were like lifelines for me when I felt completely lost.
  • Rest, Rest, Rest: Your body and mind are working overtime. You need more sleep than usual. Don't fight it. Prioritize rest. It's not laziness; it's self-preservation. This process is physically and mentally exhausting.

Look, seeing lights during a kundalini awakening is par for the course for many. It's a sign that your awareness is expanding, that your nervous system is shifting, and that you're undergoing a deep transformation. It can be beautiful, it can be terrifying, and it can be utterly disorienting. But you're not going crazy. You're not alone. And you will get through this. It's a journey, not a destination, and those lights? They're just one signpost along the way. They're telling you that you're waking up to something much bigger than you ever imagined. And honestly, that's a pretty incredible thing, even if it feels like hell right now. For a broader view of these kinds of intense, non-ordinary states, The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) is another really good resource that helps put it all in perspective. It helped me understand that I wasn't just having a spiritual crisis, but an emergence.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
